perturbation.original_text: (vi) “Personal Information” means any information about an identifiable i ndividual as defined in the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada) and include s personal health information as defined in the Personal Health Info rmation Protection Act, 2004 (Ontar io), and any amending or successor legislation to either statute and th e regulations made pursuant thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: (vi) “Personal Information” means any information about an identifiable individual, but excludes any personal health information, regardless of the definitions provided in the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada) or the Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004 (Ontario).
perturbation.explanation: The modified text explicitly excludes personal health information from the definition of Personal Information, directly contradicting the specified acts which include it.
perturbation.law_citation: PHIPA, 2004, SO 2004, c 3, Schedule A, s.4(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: PHIPA defines personal health information as a subset of personal information, whereas the contract explicitly excludes it. This misaligns with the legal definition, creating ambiguity regarding the handling of personal health information under the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text includes personal health information as part of personal information, aligning with the Personal Health Information Protection Act (PHIPA). The changed text explicitly excludes personal health information, directly contradicting the original definition and the intent of PHIPA, as personal health information is a subset of personal information under PHIPA. The scraped snippet from the Personal Health Information Protection Act, 2004, confirms the existence and scope of this Act in Ontario, reinforcing the contradiction created by the modified contract.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor agrees to defend any and all Claims that may be brought or alleged agains t IBC and/or its resp ective directors, officers, employees, agents or contractors, and indemnify and ho ld harmless IBC and its respectiv e directors, officers, employe es, agents and contractors, from and against any and all Claims or damages that may arise, by reason of any breach of this Agreement by Vendor, its personnel or contractors or from Ve ndor’s use of the Confidenti al Information including any deficiency in or other issue relating to th e use or distribution of that portion of th e PMS System that is developed using the
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor agrees to defend any and all Claims that may be brought or alleged against IBC and/or its respective directors, officers, employees, agents or contractors, and indemnify and hold harmless IBC and its respective directors, officers, employees, agents and contractors, from and against any and all Claims or damages that may arise, by reason of any breach of this Agreement by Vendor, its personnel or contractors or from Vendor’s use of the Confidential Information excluding claims arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct by IBC.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding claims arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct by IBC, the modification contradicts the general indemnity obligation. This shifts the risk and responsibility in a way that may violate laws concerning liability for gross negligence.
perturbation.law_citation: Occupiers' Liability Act, RSO 1990, c O.2
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Occupiers' Liability Act primarily concerns property safety, the general principle is that one cannot contract out of liability for gross negligence. By attempting to exclude liability for gross negligence, the contract may be unenforceable to that extent.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract attempts to exclude liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct by IBC. The Law Explanation states that a party cannot contract out of liability for gross negligence, citing the principles of the Occupiers' Liability Act. While the provided snippet from the Occupiers' Liability Act primarily concerns property safety and occupier's duty of care (section 3(1)), the general principle, as interpreted by the Law Explanation, is that one cannot contract out of liability for gross negligence. By excluding such liability, the changed text directly contradicts this principle, thus creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, and the parties further cons ent to non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal or provincial courts sitting in Toronto, Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws in force in the Province of Ontario, but disputes will be resolved through mandatory arbitration in a jurisdiction of IBC's choosing, waiving any right to court proceedings.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces mandatory arbitration and waives the right to court proceedings, potentially conflicting with laws governing contractual fairness and the right to access courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Arbitration Act permits arbitration agreements, Section 7(2) allows a court to set aside an arbitration agreement if it finds it to be unconscionable, induced by fraud, or if there was unequal bargaining power. By forcing arbitration in a location chosen by IBC, this can be seen as an unequal bargaining power and contradict the law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces mandatory arbitration and waives the right to court proceedings, potentially conflicting with laws governing contractual fairness and the right to access courts. The Law Explanation specifies that section 7(2) of the Arbitration Act allows a court to set aside an arbitration agreement if unconscionable or if there was unequal bargaining power. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Arbitration Act, 1991, S.O. 1991, c. 17) confirms this act exists and governs arbitration. By forcing arbitration in a location chosen by IBC, there could be an abuse of power, contradicting the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: (c) IBC has or expects to enter into service agreements dire ctly with Providers that will govern such Provider’s access to and use of the HCAI System. However, IBC does not guarantee availability or ope ration of the HCAI System to Vendor. Without prejudice to any right an individual Provider may have against IBC, IBC shall have no liability to Vendor in respect of any failure or problem in the operation of the HCAI System, including any communication proble ms between Providers and the HCAI System. IN NO EVENT WILL THE MEASURE OF DAMAGES PAYABLE BY EITHER PARTY INCLUDE, NOR WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR, ANY CONSEQUE NTIAL, INDIRECT, INCI DENTAL, EXEMPLARY OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATI ON, DAMAGES DUE TO BUSINESS INTERRUPTION OR LOST PROFITS, SAVINGS, COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE OR GOODWILL) ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/ OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM, REGARDLESS OF THE TYPE OF CLAIM, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING FUNDAMENTAL BREACH OR FAILURE OF THE ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY REMEDY CONTAINED HEREIN), NEGLIGENCE, STRICT LIABILIT Y OR OTHER LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY, WHETHER OR NOT FORESEEABLE, AND REGARDLESS OF THE CAUSE OF SUCH DAMAGES EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES IN ADVANCE. IBC’S LIABILITY FOR ANY MATTER RELATING TO OR ARISING FROM THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM, WHETHER BASED UPON AN ACTION OR CLAIM IN CONTRACT, WARRANTY, EQUITY, TORT, NEGLIGEN CE, OR OTHERWISE, SHALL NOT EXCEED, IN THE AGGREGATE AND IN RESPECT OF ALL ACTIONS, CLAIMS AND DAMAGES , AN AMOUNT EQUAL TO FIVE HUNDRED DOLLARS.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) IBC has or expects to enter into service agreements directly with Providers that will govern such Provider’s access to and use of the HCAI System. However, IBC does not guarantee availability or operation of the HCAI System to Vendor. Without prejudice to any right an individual Provider may have against IBC, IBC shall have no liability to Vendor in respect of any failure or problem in the operation of the HCAI System, including any communication problems between Providers and the HCAI System. IN NO EVENT WILL THE MEASURE OF DAMAGES PAYABLE BY EITHER PARTY INCLUDE, NOR WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR, ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, EXEMPLARY OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, DAMAGES DUE TO BUSINESS INTERRUPTION OR LOST PROFITS, SAVINGS, COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE OR GOODWILL) ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/ OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM, REGARDLESS OF THE TYPE OF CLAIM, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING FUNDAMENTAL BREACH OR FAILURE OF THE ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF THIS AGREEMENT OR ANY REMEDY CONTAINED HEREIN), NEGLIGENCE, STRICT LIABILITY OR OTHER LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY, WHETHER OR NOT FORESEEABLE, AND REGARDLESS OF THE CAUSE OF SUCH DAMAGES EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES IN ADVANCE. IBC’S LIABILITY FOR ANY MATTER RELATING TO OR ARISING FROM THIS AGREEMENT, THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND/OR THE OPERATION OF THE HCAI SYSTEM IS LIMITED TO ONE DOLLAR, REGARDLESS OF ANY CIRCUMSTANCES.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the liability cap to one dollar could be seen as unconscionable and contradict general principles of contractual fairness, especially if it effectively shields IBC from any meaningful responsibility for its actions.
perturbation.law_citation: Sale of Goods Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.1, s. 20
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this agreement is not primarily a sale of goods, the principle of unconscionability can be applied more broadly. Section 20 suggests implied conditions and warranties may exist, and a one-dollar liability cap could be viewed as an attempt to negate these responsibilities unfairly, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable if deemed unconscionable by a court.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the liability cap to one dollar, which the perturbation explanation suggests could be viewed as unconscionable. The law explanation references section 20 of the Sale of Goods Act, suggesting that implied conditions and warranties may exist, and the one-dollar liability cap could be viewed as an attempt to negate these responsibilities unfairly. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines various aspects of the Sale of Goods Act, including implied conditions and warranties. Although the agreement might not be primarily a sale of goods, the principle of unconscionability and implied warranties can be applied more broadly. Therefore, the modified text creates a contradiction by potentially negating implied responsibilities, supported by the snippet's reference to 'implied conditions and warranties' and the unconscionability argument.
